# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
*Explain the difference between reactive and deliberative navigation strategies. To which category does the wander-and-avoid algorithm belong?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Reactive navigation operates on **immediate local sensor data** without maintaining or using a global map of the environment. The robot responds to what it senses *right now*, making it computationally cheap and robust to dynamic changes. **Deliberative navigation**, in contrast, uses a pre-built or simultaneously-built map, plans an optimal path using algorithms such as A* or Dijkstra, and then executes the plan.

The wander-and-avoid algorithm is a **purely reactive** strategy (Brooks' Subsumption Architecture) because it makes every motion decision based solely on the current `LaserScan` reading, with no internal map, no path memory, and no global goal. This is why the experiment does not require `gmapping` or `amcl` nodes.

*(Award 1 mark for definition of reactive, 1 mark for deliberative contrast, 1 mark for correct classification with reasoning.)*

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
*What is the role of the `/cmd_vel` topic in a ROS-based mobile robot? State the message type and the two velocity components it carries.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `/cmd_vel` topic is the **standard ROS velocity command channel** through which any navigation node sends motion instructions to the robot's base controller. The message type is `geometry_msgs/Twist`, which contains two `Vector3` structures:

- `linear.x` — the forward translational velocity in **m/s**.
- `angular.z` — the rotational velocity about the vertical (z) axis in **rad/s**.

The other fields (`linear.y`, `linear.z`, `angular.x`, `angular.y`) are ignored by a differential-drive robot. The wander node computes both values at each control cycle and publishes a `Twist` message; the `gazebo_ros_diff_drive` plugin then converts it into individual wheel velocities.

*(Award 1 mark for topic role, 1 mark for message type, 1 mark for the two components with units.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete architecture of a reactive wander-and-avoid system for a TurtleBot in Gazebo. Label all ROS topics, message types, and the algorithm's three main states. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the equations used by the wander node to compute the linear and angular velocity commands from a `LaserScan` message. Use a numerical example with $r_{min} = 0.42$ m, $d_{safe} = 0.6$ m, $d_{stop} = 0.3$ m, $v_{max} = 0.22$ m/s, $\alpha^* = 0.12$ rad, and $k_\omega = 1.5$ to demonstrate the final `Twist` output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The architecture consists of three layers:

1. **Sensing Layer:** Gazebo physics engine → TurtleBot3 URDF → `gazebo_ros_laser` plugin → publishes `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` to `/scan` topic (360 samples, range 0.12 m to 3.5 m).
2. **Decision Layer:** The wander node (Python script using `rospy` or `rclpy`) subscribes to `/scan`, applies the cone filter, computes $r_{min}$ and $\alpha^*$, then runs the state machine.
3. **Actuation Layer:** Wander node publishes `geometry_msgs/Twist` to `/cmd_vel` → `gazebo_ros_diff_drive` plugin → wheel torques.

**Three main states:**
- `WANDER`: move forward at $v_{max}$ with small random $\omega$.
- `STEER`: reduce $v$, set $\omega = -k_\omega \alpha^*$.
- `EMERGENCY_TURN`: $v = 0$, rotate in place.

*(Award 2 marks for sensing layer, 2 marks for decision layer with state machine, 2 marks for actuation layer, 1 mark for topic labels and message types.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

The linear velocity scaling formula is:

$$v_{cmd} = v_{max} \cdot \frac{\max(0,\ r_{min} - d_{stop})}{d_{safe} - d_{stop}}$$

Substituting values:

$$v_{cmd} = 0.22 \cdot \frac{\max(0,\ 0.42 - 0.3)}{0.6 - 0.3} = 0.22 \cdot \frac{0.12}{0.3} = 0.22 \cdot 0.4 = 0.088 \text{ m/s}$$

The angular velocity formula is:

$$\omega_{cmd} = -k_\omega \cdot \alpha^* = -1.5 \cdot 0.12 = -0.18 \text{ rad/s}$$

Since $\vert -0.18 \vert < 2.75$ rad/s, no saturation is needed.

Final `Twist` message:

$$v_{final} = +0.088 \text{ m/s}, \quad \omega_{final} = -0.18 \text{ rad/s}$$

The robot will move forward slowly while turning gently to its right (away from the obstacle on the left, since $\alpha^* = +0.12$ rad is to the left of the forward axis).

*(Award 3 marks for correct formula statement, 2 marks for correct numerical substitution, 2 marks for final Twist values and physical interpretation.)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the role of `gazebo_ros_laser` and `gazebo_ros_diff_drive` plugins in the TurtleBot simulation. Describe the message flow from a physical obstacle to a wheel rotation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TurtleBot's LIDAR returns 720 samples covering a 270° field of view (from -135° to +135°). Compute (i) the angular increment per sample, (ii) the angular range of the safety cone in radians when $\phi_{half} = 25°$, and (iii) the number of rays that fall within the safety cone. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The `gazebo_ros_laser` plugin is attached to the TurtleBot's URDF laser link. Its role is to **simulate a 2D planar LIDAR** by casting rays within its field of view, detecting collisions with the Gazebo collision geometries, and publishing the measured ranges as a `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` message to the `/scan` topic. It bridges the virtual physics world with ROS.

The `gazebo_ros_diff_drive` plugin is attached to the robot's base footprint. It **subscribes to `/cmd_vel`**, converts the `Twist` command into left and right wheel velocities using the unicycle-to-differential-drive kinematic equations:

$$v_L = v_{cmd} - \frac{\omega_{cmd} \cdot L}{2}, \quad v_R = v_{cmd} + \frac{\omega_{cmd} \cdot L}{2}$$

where $L$ is the wheelbase. These are then applied as joint velocities to the `wheel_left_joint` and `wheel_right_joint`.

**Message flow:** Obstacle blocks virtual ray → `gazebo_ros_laser` measures range → publishes `LaserScan` to `/scan` → wander node subscribes, decides → publishes `Twist` to `/cmd_vel` → `gazebo_ros_diff_drive` computes wheel speeds → Gazebo applies torques → robot (and its sensors) move in the simulated world.

*(Award 2 marks for laser plugin role, 2 marks for diff_drive plugin role, 2 marks for kinematic equations, 1 mark for the complete message flow.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Angular increment per sample:**

$$\theta_{inc} = \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta_{min}}{N - 1} = \frac{270° - (-135°)}{720 - 1} = \frac{405°}{719} = 0.5633°$$

Converting to radians:

$$\theta_{inc} = 0.5633° \cdot \frac{\pi}{180°} = 0.00983 \text{ rad/sample}$$

**(ii) Safety cone angular range:**

Total cone width = $2 \cdot \phi_{half} = 2 \cdot 25° = 50°$

In radians:

$$S_{cone} = 50° \cdot \frac{\pi}{180°} = 0.8727 \text{ rad}$$

**(iii) Number of rays in the safety cone:**

$$N_{cone} = \left\lfloor \frac{S_{cone}}{\theta_{inc}} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{50°}{0.5633°} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 88.76 \rfloor + 1 = 88 + 1 = 89 \text{ rays}$$

*(Award 2 marks for part (i) with correct conversion, 2 marks for part (ii) with both degree and radian forms, 3 marks for part (iii) with correct floor function application and final count.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Students frequently publish $\omega$ in degrees/second to `/cmd_vel`. ROS expects **radians/second**. Always convert: $\omega_{rad} = \omega_{deg} \cdot \pi/180$.
> 2. **Off-by-one in ray indexing:** The first ray index is $i = 0$ (not 1), corresponding to `angle_min`. The $N$-th ray is at `angle_max`, not `angle_max + angle_increment`.
> 3. **Confusing $d_{safe}$ and $d_{stop}$:** $d_{safe}$ triggers deceleration; $d_{stop}$ triggers full stop and rotation. Mixing these thresholds causes oscillation.
> 4. **Forgetting to call `rospy.spin()` or `rclpy.spin()`:** The wander node will exit immediately if the spin function is missing, and the robot will not move.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The wander-and-avoid algorithm is a **purely reactive** behavior; it does NOT use a global map.
- The control loop runs at a **fixed rate (typically 10 Hz)** using a ROS `Timer` or `Rate` object.
- The `/cmd_vel` topic carries `geometry_msgs/Twist` with `linear.x` in **m/s** and `angular.z` in **rad/s**.
- The LIDAR publishes `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` to `/scan` with a configurable number of samples and angular range.
- The **safety cone** is a ±30° to ±60° forward wedge; only rays in this wedge influence the avoidance decision.
- Two critical thresholds: **$d_{safe}$** (deceleration begins) and **$d_{stop}$** (emergency stop).
- The **steering command** is $\omega = -k_\omega \alpha^*$, where $\alpha^*$ is the bearing of the closest obstacle.
- Linear velocity scales **linearly** between $d_{stop}$ and $d_{safe}$ for smooth deceleration.
- The `gazebo_ros_diff_drive` plugin converts `Twist` into individual wheel velocities using the **differential-drive kinematics** equations.
- TurtleBot3 Burger limits: $v_{max} = 0.22$ **m/s**, $\omega_{max} = 2.75$ **rad/s**.
- Always clamp computed velocities to these limits using `numpy.clip()` or equivalent.
- The complete launch typically involves: **Gazebo world launch → robot state publisher → wander node launch** — all orchestrated by a top-level `roslaunch` file.
- In a KTU practical exam, the examiner will check for: working launch files, correct topic subscriptions and publications, smooth obstacle avoidance over a 60-second test, and a written algorithm explanation in the record.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — Teleoperating TurtleBot in a Simulated World

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> *Teleoperation* is the remote control of a robot by a human operator using input devices such as a keyboard, joystick, or haptic interface. In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** framework, keyboard teleoperation is implemented by publishing **geometry_msgs/Twist** velocity commands to a topic (typically `/cmd_vel`) that a robot's motion controller subscribes to, enabling real-time motion in either a simulated environment (Gazebo) or on physical hardware.
> 
> **TurtleBot** is an open-source, low-cost, ROS-standard mobile robot platform (developed by Open Robotics, formerly OSRF). **TurtleBot3** (used in KTU labs) is a modular, ROS-native differential-drive robot that uses a Raspberry Pi/Intel Joule/Jetson Nano as its compute board and supports SLAM, navigation, and teleoperation out-of-the-box.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — "Driving a Car with a Steering Wheel, but Remotely"

Imagine you are sitting on your couch and your friend is wearing roller skates. You cannot push them physically, but you have a walkie-talkie that can tell them:
- "Move forward at **0.5 m/s**" → that's the **linear velocity** along the *x-axis*.
- "Turn left at **0.5 rad/s**" → that's the **angular velocity** around the *z-axis*.
- "Stop" → all velocities become **0**.

The **ROS teleop node** is the walkie-talkie. The **TurtleBot** is your friend on roller skates. The **Gazebo simulator** is the safe playground where you can test this without your friend falling over. The keyboard keys (`i`, `j`, `k`, `l`, `u`, `m`, `,`, `.`, `/`) are the physical buttons on your walkie-talkie. Pressing `i` doesn't *magically* move the robot — it merely **publishes a `Twist` message** on `/cmd_vel`, and the robot's controller *decides* what to do with it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabus Highlight**
> The KTU PCRAL507 lab requires you to:
> 1. Launch a **Gazebo simulation** of the TurtleBot3 world.
> 2. Start the **teleop_twist_keyboard** node (or `turtlebot3_teleop`).
> 3. Verify motion by inspecting `/cmd_vel` using `rqt_graph`, `ros2 topic echo`, or `rqt_topic`.
> 4. Stop the robot safely and shut down all nodes.

### 📐 Key Physical & Computational Quantities (Standard Metrics)

- **Linear Velocity Limit** : **0.26 m/s** (default for TurtleBot3 Burger/Waffle)
- **Angular Velocity Limit** : **1.82 rad/s** (≈ **104.3°/s**)
- **Robot Footprint** : Burger is **138 mm × 178 mm**, weight **1 kg**
- **Differential Drive Base** : Two independently driven wheels + 1 caster
- **Coordinate Frame** : REP-103 — **x-forward, y-left, z-up, right-handed**
- **Standard Teleop Topic** : `/cmd_vel` of type `geometry_msgs/msg/Twist` (ROS 2) or `geometry_msgs/Twist` (ROS 1)

> [!TIP]
> Always confirm the **ROS 1 vs ROS 2** distribution installed in your KTU lab. The 2024 scheme typically uses **ROS 2 Humble/Iron/Jazzy** on Ubuntu 22.04.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The `Twist` Message — The Language of Robot Motion

A `Twist` message in ROS has **6 fields**, but for a *planar differential-drive mobile robot* like TurtleBot, only **3 fields** are actively used. The message is defined as:

```text
# Linear velocities (m/s) - movement in x, y, z directions
float64 linear.x      # ← forward (+) / backward (-) speed
float64 linear.y      # ← ALWAYS 0 for differential drive (non-holonomic constraint)
float64 linear.z      # ← ALWAYS 0 (robot cannot fly)

# Angular velocities (rad/s) - rotation around x, y, z axes
float64 angular.x     # ← ALWAYS 0 (no roll)
float64 angular.y     # ← ALWAYS 0 (no pitch)
float64 angular.z     # ← left (+) / right (-) yaw rate
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `linear.y` is always zero for TurtleBot:**
> TurtleBot is a *non-holonomic* robot — it has 3 degrees of freedom in 2D space (x, y, θ) but only 2 controllable inputs (v, ω). This is called the **unicycle model** of motion.

## 2.2 The Unicycle Kinematic Model — Step-by-Step Logic

### 🧠 The "Why" Behind the Equations

If you tell the robot to move forward at velocity *v* and turn at rate *ω*, what happens to its actual position in the world? The robot has a heading (an angle θ it is facing). Its forward motion is therefore *projected* along its heading, not along the global x-axis.

### 🛠️ The "How" — Stepwise Derivation

Given:
- `v` = linear velocity (m/s) along robot's *local* forward axis
- `ω` = angular velocity (rad/s) around robot's *local* z-axis
- `(x, y, θ)` = current pose in the world frame

Then the **world-frame velocities** of the robot are obtained by rotating the local frame by angle θ:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}(t) &= v(t) \cdot \cos(\theta(t)) \\
\dot{y}(t) &= v(t) \cdot \sin(\theta(t)) \\
\dot{\theta}(t) &= \omega(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **forward kinematics of a unicycle** (also called the **Ackermann-steering-like model** in its simplest form).

### 🔁 Discretized Update (what the simulator actually does at every time step)

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + v_k \cdot \cos(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k + v_k \cdot \sin(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

where **Δt** is the simulation step (typically **0.01 s** = 100 Hz in Gazebo).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

| # | Concept | Symbol | Equation / Value | Units | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---------|--------|------------------|-------|--------------|
| 1 | Forward velocity | $v$ | `linear.x` in `Twist` | **m/s** | Direct input from keyboard |
| 2 | Yaw rate | $\omega$ | `angular.z` in `Twist` | **rad/s** | Direct input from keyboard |
| 3 | Unicycle X-vel | $\dot{x}$ | $v \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | m/s | World-frame speed along X |
| 4 | Unicycle Y-vel | $\dot{y}$ | $v \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | m/s | World-frame speed along Y |
| 5 | Heading rate | $\dot{\theta}$ | $\omega$ | rad/s | World-frame rotation |
| 6 | Discrete update X | $x_{k+1}$ | $x_k + v_k \cos(\theta_k) \Delta t$ | m | Odometry integration |
| 7 | Discrete update Y | $y_{k+1}$ | $y_k + v_k \sin(\theta_k) \Delta t$ | m | Odometry integration |
| 8 | Discrete heading | $\theta_{k+1}$ | $\theta_k + \omega_k \Delta t$ | rad | Odometry integration |
| 9 | Wheel radius (Burger) | $r$ | **0.033** | m | Hardware constant |
| 10 | Wheel separation | $L$ | **0.16** | m | Hardware constant |
| 11 | Linear vel from wheels | $v$ | $\frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ | m/s | Inverse diff-drive kinematics |
| 12 | Angular vel from wheels | $\omega$ | $\frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | rad/s | Inverse diff-drive kinematics |
| 13 | Max linear (default) | $v_{max}$ | **0.26** | m/s | TurtleBot3 Burger |
| 14 | Max angular (default) | $\omega_{max}$ | **1.82** | rad/s | TurtleBot3 Burger |
| 15 | Topic name | -- | `/cmd_vel` | -- | Standard ROS convention |
| 16 | Message type | -- | `geometry_msgs/msg/Twist` | -- | Standard ROS 2 message |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition (Non-Holonomic Constraint)**
> For TurtleBot: $\dot{y}_{local} = 0$ in the robot's body frame. The robot **cannot move sideways** like a crab — it must turn first, then move. This is the *meaning* of being non-holonomic.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Teleoperation Matters |
|--------------------|---------------------------|
| **Search & Rescue** (DARPA SubT) | Operator drives the robot into a collapsed mine; teleop provides a safety override. |
| **Warehouse AMRs** (Amazon Kiva) | Initial map and edge-case recoveries are teleoperated. |
| **Space Exploration** (NASA's Robonaut) | Astronauts teleoperate rovers on Mars with time-delayed teleop. |
| **Surgical Robotics** (da Vinci) | Master-slave teleop with haptic feedback. |
| **KTU Lab** | Foundation experiment for SLAM, navigation, and obstacle avoidance. |

> [!TIP]
> The KTU examiner often asks: *"Why is direct wheel-velocity control not used in teleop?"* Answer: *Because teleop operates at the velocity-command level (Twist), abstracting the wheel dynamics — this is the **encapsulation principle** of ROS.*

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Wheel Speeds to Twist Commands (Inverse Kinematics)

We need to derive the relationship between the **two wheel angular velocities** ($\omega_L$, $\omega_R$) and the **Twist** components ($v$, $\omega$) sent to the robot.

### Step A — Define the geometric parameters
- $r$ = wheel radius = **0.033 m** (TurtleBot3 Burger)
- $L$ = distance between the two wheels = **0.16 m**
- $v_L = r \cdot \omega_L$ = linear velocity of left wheel at contact point
- $v_R = r \cdot \omega_R$ = linear velocity of right wheel at contact point

### Step B — Compute the robot's centroid linear velocity

The robot's center moves at the *average* of the two wheel contact-point velocities (rigid body assumption):

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} \\
  &= \frac{r \cdot \omega_R + r \cdot \omega_L}{2} \\
  &= \frac{r}{2} \cdot (\omega_R + \omega_L)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step C — Compute the robot's angular velocity

For pure rotation about the Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC), the linear speed at distance $L/2$ from the ICC is $\omega \cdot (L/2)$. This equals the *difference* of the two wheel speeds divided by 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega \cdot \frac{L}{2} &= \frac{v_R - v_L}{2} \\
\omega &= \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} \\
        &= \frac{r \cdot \omega_R - r \cdot \omega_L}{L} \\
        &= \frac{r}{L} \cdot (\omega_R - \omega_L)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step D — Final Inverse Kinematics Matrix (in matrix form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r}{2} & \frac{r}{2} \\ \frac{r}{L} & -\frac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic for Step D**: The matrix on the right is called the **Jacobian inverse** of the differential-drive robot. It maps *wheel speeds* to *body velocities*. The **forward Jacobian** (used by simulators) inverts this matrix and maps *body velocities* to *wheel speeds*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Numerical Example: A 3-Second Teleop Burst

**Given**: Operator presses the **'i' key** for 3 seconds. The `teleop_twist_keyboard` node is configured with:
- Forward speed: `linear.x = 0.5 m/s`
- Turn speed: `angular.z = 0.0 rad/s`
- Initial pose: $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$
- Time step: $\Delta t = 0.1$ s (for illustration)

**Find**: Final pose after 3 s.

### Step 1 — Identify the Twist values
- $v = 0.5$ m/s
- $\omega = 0.0$ rad/s

### Step 2 — Apply discrete update for each step
Since $\omega = 0$, the heading $\theta$ remains **0** for all time steps.

**Step k=1** (t = 0.1 s):
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= x_0 + v \cdot \cos(\theta_0) \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 0.5 \cdot \cos(0) \cdot 0.1 = 0.05 \text{ m} \\
y_1 &= y_0 + v \cdot \sin(\theta_0) \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 0.5 \cdot \sin(0) \cdot 0.1 = 0.00 \text{ m} \\
\theta_1 &= \theta_0 + \omega \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 0.0 \cdot 0.1 = 0 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step k=2** (t = 0.2 s):
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_2 &= 0.05 + 0.5 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.1 = 0.10 \text{ m} \\
y_2 &= 0.00 \text{ m} \\
\theta_2 &= 0 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Generalize for n steps
Since $\theta$ is constant, $x_n = n \cdot v \cdot \Delta t$ and $y_n = 0$.

**At t = 3 s, n = 30 steps**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{30} &= 30 \times 0.5 \times 0.1 = 1.5 \text{ m} \\
y_{30} &= 0 \text{ m} \\
\theta_{30} &= 0 \text{ rad} \; (0°)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result interpretation**: The robot moved **1.5 m straight forward** along the world x-axis with no rotation. If the operator had pressed **'i' + 'j'** simultaneously, the robot would have moved in a circular arc — verify this by setting $\omega \neq 0$ in the same equations.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the `geometry_msgs/msg/Twist` message structure and explain which fields are used for controlling a TurtleBot3 in teleoperation.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The `Twist` message consists of two `Vector3` sub-messages — `linear` and `angular` — each with `x`, `y`, `z` fields of type `float64` (units **m/s** and **rad/s** respectively).
> 
> For TurtleBot3, only **two fields are actively used**:
> 1. `linear.x` — controls forward / backward velocity along the robot's longitudinal axis.
> 2. `angular.z` — controls left / right yaw rate about the robot's vertical axis.
> 
> All other fields (`linear.y`, `linear.z`, `angular.x`, `angular.y`) are **always zero** because TurtleBot3 is a **differential-drive non-holonomic robot** that cannot translate sideways, vertically, or roll/pitch in place. **(3 marks: 1 for structure, 1 for the two used fields, 1 for the constraint explanation)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between `/cmd_vel` and `/odom` topics in ROS 2. Why is `/cmd_vel` a "command" topic and `/odom` a "measurement" topic?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Aspect | `/cmd_vel` | `/odom` |
> |--------|------------|---------|
> | Direction | Publisher → Controller (input) | Controller → Consumer (output) |
> | Type | `geometry_msgs/msg/Twist` | `nav_msgs/msg/Odometry` |
> | Role | Desired velocity *requested* by the operator | Actual pose *measured* from encoders / IMU |
> | Producer | Teleop node (human) | `ros2_control` + EKF / wheel odometry |
> 
> **`/cmd_vel`** carries the *operator's intent* (a command). **`/odom`** carries the *robot's report* of where it actually is (a measurement). They are not interchangeable; the controller continuously compares them to detect slip, drift, or external disturbances. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - ❌ Do **not** mix ROS 1 (`rostopic`) and ROS 2 (`ros2 topic`) commands in the same answer.
> - ❌ Do **not** say "TurtleBot uses `linear.y` for sideways motion" — it is non-holonomic.
> - ❌ Do **not** omit the **units** (m/s, rad/s) in equations; marks are deducted for unitless answers.
> - ❌ Do **not** confuse `/odom` (measurement) with `/cmd_vel` (command) — 2–3 marks are lost here frequently.
> - ❌ Do **not** forget to **export `TURTLEBOT3_MODEL`** when launching Gazebo.

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the ROS 2 architecture for keyboard teleoperation of a TurtleBot3 in Gazebo. Identify the publishers, subscribers, topics, and message types involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TurtleBot3 Burger (wheel radius `r = 0.033 m`, wheel separation `L = 0.16 m`) receives a Twist command `linear.x = 0.4 m/s` and `angular.z = 0.5 rad/s` for 4 seconds. Starting from pose `(0, 0, 0)`, compute the final pose using the discrete unicycle model with `Δt = 0.1 s`. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 marks

| Step | Content | Marks |
|------|---------|-------|
| 1 | **Architecture diagram** with four blocks: Keyboard → `teleop_twist_keyboard` node → `/cmd_vel` topic → `diff_drive_controller` → Gazebo simulation | **2** |
| 2 | **Publisher identification**: `teleop_twist_keyboard` publishes `geometry_msgs/msg/Twist` on `/cmd_vel` | **1** |
| 3 | **Subscriber identification**: `diff_drive_controller` (part of `ros2_control`) subscribes to `/cmd_vel` and converts Twist → wheel joint velocities using the **inverse Jacobian** | **2** |
| 4 | **Feedback path**: Gazebo → `robot_state_publisher` → publishes `/odom` (Odometry) and `/tf` | **1** |
| 5 | **Visualization**: RViz2 subscribes to `/odom`, `/scan`, `/tf` for live display | **1** |

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 marks

**Given**: $r = 0.033$ m, $L = 0.16$ m, $v = 0.4$ m/s, $\omega = 0.5$ rad/s, $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$, $T = 4$ s, $\Delta t = 0.1$ s.

**Number of steps**: $n = T / \Delta t = 40$ steps.

**Step 1 — Write the discrete unicycle update equations**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + v \cdot \cos(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k + v \cdot \sin(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the heading update (closed form)**:
Since $\omega$ is constant,
$$
\theta_{40} = \theta_0 + \omega \cdot T = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 4 = 2.0 \text{ rad} \approx 114.6°
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the closed-form path integral**:
For constant $v$ and $\omega$, the discrete sums converge to:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x(T) &= \int_0^T v \cdot \cos(\omega t) \, dt = \frac{v}{\omega} \sin(\omega T) \\
y(T) &= \int_0^T v \cdot \sin(\omega t) \, dt = \frac{v}{\omega} (1 - \cos(\omega T))
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerically**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{v}{\omega} &= \frac{0.4}{0.5} = 0.8 \text{ m} \\
\omega T &= 0.5 \cdot 4 = 2.0 \text{ rad} \\
\sin(2.0) &\approx 0.9093 \\
\cos(2.0) &\approx -0.4161
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(4) &= 0.8 \cdot 0.9093 = 0.7274 \text{ m} \\
y(4) &= 0.8 \cdot (1 - (-0.4161)) = 0.8 \cdot 1.4161 = 1.1329 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final pose**: $(x, y, \theta) = (0.727 \text{ m}, \; 1.133 \text{ m}, \; 2.0 \text{ rad})$

**Verification via discrete sum (Step 5)**:
| Step k | θ_k (rad) | cos θ | sin θ | Δx | Δy |
|--------|-----------|-------|-------|-----|-----|
| 0 | 0.00 | 1.000 | 0.000 | 0.040 | 0.000 |
| 10 | 0.50 | 0.878 | 0.479 | 0.035 | 0.019 |
| 20 | 1.00 | 0.540 | 0.841 | 0.022 | 0.034 |
| 30 | 1.50 | 0.071 | 0.998 | 0.003 | 0.040 |
| 40 | 2.00 | -0.416 | 0.909 | -0.017 | 0.036 |

Cumulative sums ≈ (0.727, 1.133) ✓ **Matches closed-form result.**

| Distribution | Marks |
|--------------|-------|
| Equations (Step 1) | **1** |
| Heading update (Step 2) | **1** |
| Path integral (Step 3) | **2** |
| Numerical evaluation (Step 4) | **2** |
| Final answer + units (Step 5) | **1** |

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the role of the **`teleop_twist_keyboard`** node in ROS 2. Provide the default key-to-Twist mapping and describe how the *linear scale* and *angular scale* parameters modify the published velocities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a TurtleBot3 Burger, derive the **inverse differential-drive kinematics** mapping wheel angular velocities $(\omega_L, \omega_R)$ to the Twist components $(v, \omega)$. If the robot publishes a Twist of $v = 0.22$ m/s, $\omega = 1.5$ rad/s, compute the required wheel angular velocities. Use $r = 0.033$ m, $L = 0.16$ m. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 marks

**Role of the node (2 marks)**: The `teleop_twist_keyboard` node is a generic ROS 2 package (not specific to TurtleBot) that translates raw keyboard scancodes into `geometry_msgs/msg/Twist` messages published on a topic (default `/cmd_vel`). It enables a human operator to *manually drive* any compatible robot without writing custom control code.

**Default key map (3 marks)**:

| Key | linear.x | angular.z | Description |
|-----|----------|-----------|-------------|
| `i` | +1 | 0 | Forward |
| `,` | -1 | 0 | Backward |
| `j` | 0 | +1 | Rotate CCW (left) |
| `l` | 0 | -1 | Rotate CW (right) |
| `u` | +1 | +1 | Forward + left |
| `o` | +1 | -1 | Forward + right |
| `m` | -1 | -1 | Backward + right |
| `.` | -1 | +1 | Backward + left |
| `k` | 0 | 0 | **STOP** |
| `q`/`z` | scale ±10% | -- | Adjust linear speed |
| `w`/`x` | -- | scale ±10% | Adjust angular speed |
| `Ctrl-C` | -- | -- | Quit node |

**Scale mechanism (2 marks)**: The published `linear.x` is `(key value) × (current linear scale)`, and `angular.z` is `(key value) × (current angular scale)`. The scales start at defaults (commonly `0.5` m/s and `1.0` rad/s) and are adjusted with `q/z` and `w/x`. This is why the same `i` key produces different speeds at different times. The operator uses `k` to stop, which is **critical for safety**.

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 marks

**Derivation (4 marks)** — repeated here for self-contained answering:

Let $r$ = wheel radius, $L$ = wheelbase, $\omega_L, \omega_R$ = wheel angular velocities, $v_L = r \omega_L$, $v_R = r \omega_R$.

Robot center's linear velocity is the average of the two wheel contact-point velocities:
$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

The angular velocity equals the rate of change of the angle between the two wheels as seen from the ICC:
$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

In matrix form:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r/2 & r/2 \\ r/L & -r/L \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Solve for wheel velocities (forward kinematics) (2 marks)** — invert the matrix:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_R &= \frac{1}{r}\left(v + \frac{\omega L}{2}\right) \\
\omega_L &= \frac{1}{r}\left(v - \frac{\omega L}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical evaluation (1 mark)**:
Given $v = 0.22$ m/s, $\omega = 1.5$ rad/s, $r = 0.033$ m, $L = 0.16$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega R_{\text{term}} &= \frac{\omega L}{2} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.16}{2} = 0.12 \text{ m/s} \\
\omega_R &= \frac{1}{0.033}(0.22 + 0.12) = \frac{0.34}{0.033} \approx 10.30 \text{ rad/s} \\
\omega_L &= \frac{1}{0.033}(0.22 - 0.12) = \frac{0.10}{0.033} \approx 3.03 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final answer**: $\omega_R \approx 10.30$ rad/s (≈ **98.7 RPM**), $\omega_L \approx 3.03$ rad/s (≈ **28.9 RPM**). The right wheel must spin faster than the left, producing a left turn as expected for positive $\omega$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Teleoperation** = remote manual control via published velocity messages.
- [x] The single most important topic is **`/cmd_vel`** of type `geometry_msgs/msg/Twist`.
- [x] **Only two Twist fields** matter for TurtleBot: `linear.x` (m/s) and `angular.z` (rad/s).
- [x] TurtleBot3 is **differential-drive & non-holonomic** → `linear.y` is structurally zero.
- [x] The **unicycle kinematic model** governs motion:
  - $\dot{x} = v \cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v \sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$.
- [x] **Inverse differential-drive kinematics**:
  - $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$, $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$.
- [x] **Forward differential-drive kinematics** (used by the controller):
  - $\omega_R = \frac{1}{r}(v + \frac{\omega L}{2})$, $\omega_L = \frac{1}{r}(v - \frac{\omega L}{2})$.
- [x] **Hardware constants for Burger**: $r = 0.033$ m, $L = 0.16$ m, $v_{max} = 0.26$ m/s, $\omega_{max} = 1.82$ rad/s.
- [x] **Key map**: `i` (forward), `,` (back), `j` (left), `l` (right), `k` (STOP), `q/z` and `w/x` (scales).
- [x] **Always `export TURTLEBOT3_MODEL=burger`** (or `waffle`, `waffle_pi`) before launching.
- [x] **ROS 2 commands** (not `rostopic`): `ros2 topic list`, `ros2 topic echo /cmd_vel`, `ros2 run rqt_graph rqt_graph`, `ros2 node list`.
- [x] **`/cmd_vel` = command**; **`/odom` = measurement** — never confuse them.
- [x] **Launch sequence** for KTU lab: (1) source ROS, (2) export model, (3) `ros2 launch turtlebot3_gazebo …`, (4) `ros2 run teleop_twist_keyboard …` in a new terminal.
- [x] **REP-103 coordinate convention**: x-forward, y-left, z-up (right-handed) for ground robots.
- [x] **Path traced under constant Twist** is a **circular arc** (not a straight line) unless $\omega = 0$.
- [x] **Common slide-deck / answer keywords** that fetch full marks:
  - "non-holonomic constraint", "unicycle model", "REP-103", "inverse Jacobian", "`ros2_control`", "`diff_drive_controller`", "differential-drive platform".

> [!TIP]
> **One-line exam mnemonic**: **"T2, L, W"** → **Twist-2 fields** (linear.x, angular.z), **L**-shape frame (REP-103), **W**heels mapped via inverse Jacobian. Master this and you master Module Experiment 2.

topics.title: Simulate the turtlebot robot that follows a line
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAL507 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Simulate the turtlebot robot that follows a line # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Line Following Simulation** is a classical mobile-robotics experiment in which a differential-drive platform (TurtleBot 2/3) autonomously tracks a continuous, high-contrast path drawn on the floor using **onboard vision** and **closed-loop feedback control**. The path is detected as a pixel-region in a 2D image stream, the **centroid offset** from the image center is computed as the control error $e(t)$, and a controller converts that error into angular velocity commands that drive the wheel motors. In the **KTU ROS Lab** context, this experiment validates three core competencies: 1. **Publisher/Subscriber architecture** in ROS 2 (Humble/Iron). 2. **Sensor-to-actuator pipeline** using `image_proc`, `cv_bridge`, and `/cmd_vel` topics. 3. **PID control** implemented in a Python node (or C++). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Skill Outcome (CO2):** *Design and implement ROS nodes for perception, planning, and control of a simulated mobile robot.* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "A Blind Man Walking on a Yellow Carpet" Imagine a person who can **only see directly in front of them** through a narrow window. A bright yellow carpet is laid on the floor. To walk straight along it: 1. The person tilts their head left/right to **center the carpet** in the window. 2. If the carpet shifts **left** in the view, the brain commands the **right foot** to push harder → the body curves back right. 3. The brain keeps doing this every fraction of a second. TurtleBot does the **exact same thing**, but: - The "window" is the **RGB camera** at 30 FPS. - The "yellow carpet" is the **dark line on light floor** (or vice-versa). - The "brain" is your **ROS 2 node running the PID controller**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Performance Metrics (bold):** > - **Linear velocity** $v$ = **0.2 m/s** > - **Angular velocity bounds** $\omega \in [-1.0, +1.0]$ **rad/s** > - **Image resolution** = **640 × 480 px** > - **Control loop rate** = **10 Hz** (default) --- ## 1.3 Required Software Stack | Component | Package | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Gazebo Simulator | `gazebo_ros_pkgs` | Physics + 3-D rendering | | TurtleBot 3 | `turtlebot3_gazebo`, `turtlebot3_description` | URDF model + plugins | | ROS 2 Bridge | `ros_gz_bridge` (Humble) | Topic conversion | | Computer Vision | `opencv-python`, `cv_bridge` | Image processing | | Control | Custom Python node | PID logic | | World Plugin | `turtlebot3_world` | Pre-built environment | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Line-Following Control Pipeline The robot's decision-making is decomposed into a **6-stage pipeline**, every cycle (every $T_s$ seconds): 1. **Image Acquisition** — Subscriber receives `sensor_msgs/msg/Image` from `/camera/image_raw`. 2. **Color Segmentation** — Convert BGR → HSV, threshold the line color into a binary mask. 3. **Centroid Extraction** — Compute the $(x, y)$ centroid of the white pixels using `cv2.moments`. 4. **Error Calculation** — Subtract centroid $x$ from the image's horizontal center $x_c$: $$e(t) = x_c - x_{\text{centroid}}$$ 5. **PID Computation** — Convert error into angular velocity $\omega(t)$. 6. **Velocity Publishing** — Publish `Twist` message to `/cmd_vel`. > [!TIP] > The line "tells" the robot where it is — the robot does **not** know the map. It only reacts to what it sees **right now**. This is a **reactive (behavior-based)** control strategy, not SLAM. --- ## 2.2 Why PID and Not Just "P"? A **proportional-only** controller causes **oscillation** because the robot overshoots the line repeatedly. The **derivative term** damps this oscillation, and the **integral term** removes steady-state drift on curves. The **discrete-time PID** law used in the node: $$\omega(t) = -K_p \, e(t) - K_d \frac{e(t) - e(t-1)}{T_s} - K_i \sum_{k=0}^{t} e(k)\, T_s$$ > [!WARNING] > The **negative sign** is critical. If the line drifts to the **left** of center (positive error), the robot must rotate **clockwise** to follow it — and clockwise is **negative $\omega$** in ROS (`right-hand rule`). --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value | Units | |---|---|---|---| | $v$ | Forward linear velocity | $0.2$ | m/s | | $\omega$ | Angular velocity | $-1.0$ to $+1.0$ | rad/s | | $e(t)$ | Lateral pixel error | $-320$ to $+320$ | px | | $x_c$ | Image horizontal center | $320$ (for 640-wide image) | px | | $T_s$ | Sampling period | $0.1$ (at 10 Hz) | s | | $K_p$ | Proportional gain | $0.005$ | rad·s/(px) | | $K_d$ | Derivative gain | $0.003$ | rad·s²/px | | $K_i$ | Integral gain | $0.0001$ | rad/(px·s) | | $f_{\text{fps}}$ | Camera frame rate | $30$ | Hz | | $A_{\text{mask}}$ | White pixel count | varies | px² | > [!IMPORTANT] > Boundary condition for **saturation**: $\omega = \operatorname{clip}(\omega, -\omega_{\max}, +\omega_{\max})$ where **$\omega_{\max} = 1.0$ rad/s**. Exceeding this makes Gazebo's differential-drive model unstable. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Utility Line-following AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) are the **backbone of warehouse automation** — Amazon, DHL, and Ocado use fleets of similar robots to move totes. The same perception-then-control loop appears in: - **Autonomous lane-keeping** in cars (Tesla Autopilot, Mobileye). - **Endoscopy capsules** that follow the intestinal tract. - **Harvesting robots** that follow crop rows. - **Airport baggage tractors** on painted taxiways. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Centroid Error Starting from the binary mask $M(x, y) \in \{0, 1\}$ produced after HSV thresholding, the **image moments** are defined (per OpenCV `cv2.moments`) as: $$M_{ij} = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} x^{i} y^{j} M(x, y)$$ The **zeroth moment** $M_{00}$ equals the number of white pixels (the mask area). The **first moment** $M_{10}$ is the sum of all $x$-coordinates of white pixels. The centroid's $x$-coordinate is therefore: $$x_{\text{centroid}} = \frac{M_{10}}{M_{00}}$$ ### Step-by-step expansion: $$ \begin{aligned} M_{00} &= \sum_{x=0}^{W-1}\sum_{y=0}^{H-1} M(x, y) \\[4pt] M_{10} &= \sum_{x=0}^{W-1}\sum_{y=0}^{H-1} x \cdot M(x, y) \\[4pt] x_{\text{centroid}} &= \frac{M_{10}}{M_{00}} \quad \text{(only valid if } M_{00} > 0\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ The **lateral error** relative to the image's center $x_c = W/2$ is then: $$ \begin{aligned} e(t) &= x_c - x_{\text{centroid}} \\[4pt] e(t) &= \frac{W}{2} - \frac{M_{10}}{M_{00}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > If $M_{00} = 0$ (no line detected), the previous error value must be **held** (memory action) or the robot must execute a **search behavior** (rotate in place). This is the **NaN/divide-by-zero** pitfall examiners check for. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Discrete PID Law Starting from the continuous-time PID law: $$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$ Apply **backward-Euler discretization** with sampling period $T_s$: $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau &\;\approx\; T_s \sum_{k=0}^{n} e(k) \quad \text{(rectangular rule)} \\[4pt] \frac{de(t)}{dt} &\;\approx\; \frac{e(n) - e(n-1)}{T_s} \quad \text{(backward difference)} \end{aligned} $$ Substitute into the continuous law and negate for correct steering sign: $$ \begin{aligned} \omega(n) &= -K_p e(n) - K_i T_s \sum_{k=0}^{n} e(k) - K_d \frac{e(n) - e(n-1)}{T_s} \end{aligned} $$ This is the exact formula implemented in the Python node in §3.4. --- ## 3.3 Tuning Heuristic (Ziegler–Nicholas-like) For a beginner, gains can be initialized using the rule-of-thumb: $$ \begin{aligned} K_p &= \frac{\omega_{\max}}{e_{\max}} = \frac{1.0}{320} \approx 0.003 \\[4pt] K_d &\approx \frac{K_p \cdot T_s}{0.5} = 0.003 \times 0.1 \times 2 = 0.0006 \\[4pt] K_i &\approx 0 \end{aligned} $$ Start with $K_i = 0$, raise $K_p$ until the robot wiggles, then add $K_d$ to stop the wiggle. --- ## 3.4 Full Working ROS 2 Python Implementation The following node is the **complete, runnable** script. Save it as `line_follower.py` inside `~/ros2_ws/src/turtlebot3_line_follower/turtlebot3_line_follower/`. ```python import rclpy from rclpy.node import Node from sensor_msgs.msg import Image from geometry_msgs.msg import Twist from cv_bridge import CvBridge import cv2 import numpy as np class LineFollower(Node): def __init__(self): super().__init__('line_follower') self.bridge = CvBridge() # Subscribe to camera topic exposed by Gazebo self.subscription = self.create_subscription( Image, '/camera/image_raw', self.listener_callback, 10) # Publish velocity commands to the diff-drive plugin self.publisher_ = self.create_publisher(Twist, '/cmd_vel', 10) # PID state variables self.prev_error = 0.0 self.integral = 0.0 # Control parameters (tuned for TurtleBot 3 Burger) self.Kp = 0.005 self.Kd = 0.003 self.Ki = 0.0001 self.dt = 0.1 # 10 Hz loop self.linear_vel = 0.2 self.angular_max = 1.0 # Image center (computed dynamically if needed) self.image_center = 320 # for 640x480 self.get_logger().info("Line Follower Node Started") def listener_callback(self, msg): try: cv_image = self.bridge.imgmsg_to_cv2(msg, desired_encoding='bgr8') except Exception as e: self.get_logger().error(f"cv_bridge exception: {e}") return # ---- 1. Preprocess: blur, convert to HSV, threshold ---- hsv = cv2.cvtColor(cv_image, cv2.COLOR_BGR2HSV) blur = cv2.GaussianBlur(hsv, (5, 5), 0) # Threshold for a YELLOW line (Hue 20-30 in OpenCV HSV) lower_yellow = np.array([20, 100, 100]) upper_yellow = np.array([30, 255, 255]) mask = cv2.inRange(blur, lower_yellow, upper_yellow) # ---- 2. Centroid extraction ---- M = cv2.moments(mask) twist = Twist() if M['m00'] > 0: cx = int(M['m10'] / M['m00']) cy = int(M['m01'] / M['m00']) # ---- 3. Compute lateral error ---- error = self.image_center - cx # ---- 4. PID controller ---- self.integral += error * self.dt derivative = (error - self.prev_error) / self.dt omega = -(self.Kp * error + self.Ki * self.integral + self.Kd * derivative) # Saturate angular velocity omega = np.clip(omega, -self.angular_max, self.angular_max) self.prev_error = error twist.linear.x = self.linear_vel twist.angular.z = float(omega) # ---- 5. Debug visualization (optional) ---- cv2.circle(cv_image, (cx, cy), 5, (0, 0, 255), -1) cv2.imshow("Camera View", cv_image) cv2.waitKey(1) else: # No line found: stop and search self.get_logger().warn("Line lost! Searching...") twist.linear.x = 0.0 twist.angular.z = 0.3 # gentle rotate to re-acquire self.publisher_.publish(twist) def main(args=None): rclpy.init(args=args) node = LineFollower() try: rclpy.spin(node) except KeyboardInterrupt: pass finally: node.destroy_node() rclpy.shutdown() cv2.destroyAllWindows() if __name__ == '__main__': main() ``` ### 3.5 Launch Sequence (KTU Viva Question) ```bash # Terminal 1 - Launch simulation export TURTLEBOT3_MODEL=burger ros2 launch turtlebot3_gazebo turtlebot3_world.launch.py # Terminal 2 - Run the line follower ros2 run turtlebot3_line_follower line_follower # Terminal 3 - Visualize the topic graph ros2 run rqt_graph rqt_graph ``` ### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Approach | Pros | Cons | KTU Mark Allocation | |---|---|---|---| | **Color Threshold (HSV)** | Simple, fast | Sensitive to lighting | Full 5 marks (basic) | | **Canny Edge + Hough** | Robust to color | Slower, complex | Bonus 2 marks | | **Deep Learning (CNN)** | Learns from data | Needs GPU + dataset | Out of syllabus | | **LIDAR-based** | Lighting-invariant | Needs costmap + extra sensor | Out of syllabus for this exp | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR GazeboWorld[Gazebo World with Line Texture] -->|renders| CameraPlugin CameraPlugin[/camera/image_raw
sensor_msgsImage/] --> CvBridge CvBridge -->|cv_image| HSVThresh[HSV Threshold Mask] HSVThresh --> MomentsCalc[cv2.moments Centroid] MomentsCalc --> ErrorCalc[Lateral Error e_t] ErrorCalc --> PID[PID Controller Node] PID -->|/cmd_vel
geometry_msgsTwist/| DiffDrive[Diff Drive Plugin] DiffDrive --> Wheels[Left and Right Wheels] Wheels -->|motion| GazeboWorld ErrorCalc --> DebugViz[OpenCV Debug Window] ``` ## 4.2 ROS 2 Topic & Node Interaction Graph ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph GazeboSim[Gazebo Simulator] camPub["/camera/image_raw
Publisher: gazebo_ros_camera"] cmdSub["/cmd_vel
Subscriber: gazebo_ros_diff_drive"] end subgraph LineFollowerNode[line_follower Node] imgSub["Subscription: Image"] cvProc["OpenCV Pipeline"] pidCtrl["PID Logic"] velPub["Publisher: Twist"] end camPub -->|Image msg| imgSub imgSub --> cvProc cvProc --> pidCtrl pidCtrl --> velPub velPub -->|Twist msg| cmdSub ``` ## 4.3 State Machine for Line-Tracking Behavior ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Searching Searching --> Tracking : Line detected M00 greater than 0 Tracking --> Tracking : Error within bounds Tracking --> Searching : Line lost for N frames Tracking --> Overtaking : Sharp curve omega greater than 0.8 Overtaking --> Tracking : Error reduced Searching --> [*] : User kills node Tracking --> [*] : User kills node ``` ## 4.4 Image-Processing Pipeline (Visual Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw BGR Image 640x480] --> B[GaussianBlur 5x5] B --> C[BGR to HSV] C --> D[inRange Yellow 20 30] D --> E[Binary Mask 0 or 255] E --> F{cv2.moments M00 greater than 0} F -- Yes --> G[Compute cx cy] F -- No --> H[Search Rotate] G --> I[error = 320 - cx] I --> J[PID output omega] J --> K[Publish /cmd_vel] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** ### Q1. What is the role of the `cv_bridge` package in a ROS 2 line-following node? **Model Answer (3 marks):** `cv_bridge` acts as a translator between ROS 2's standard `sensor_msgs/msg/Image` message type and OpenCV's native `numpy.ndarray` (BGR or grayscale) format. (1 mark) Without it, the raw image data (a byte buffer) cannot be directly manipulated using `cv2` functions. (1 mark) In the line-following pipeline, `imgmsg_to_cv2()` converts the incoming `/camera/image_raw` message into a BGR8 matrix, which is then converted to HSV for color thresholding. (1 mark) ### Q2. Why is a PID controller preferred over a simple proportional controller for line following? **Model Answer (3 marks):** A pure P-controller causes the robot to oscillate around the line because the proportional term alone cannot damp the system. (1 mark) The derivative term $K_d \cdot \dot{e}$ predicts the future error and applies a counter-torque to reduce overshoot, stabilizing the motion. (1 mark) The integral term $K_i \int e\,dt$ accumulates past errors to eliminate steady-state drift, especially when following wide curves. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer with Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete perception-decision-action pipeline for a TurtleBot line-following simulation in Gazebo. List all ROS 2 topics and message types involved. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The pipeline consists of three macro-stages (3 marks for diagram + 4 marks for explanation): 1. **Perception (Gazebo → Node):** The Gazebo `gazebo_ros_camera` plugin publishes images on `/camera/image_raw` of type `sensor_msgs/msg/Image` (encoding `bgr8`, 640×480 @ 30 Hz). (1 mark) 2. **Decision (Python Node):** The subscriber receives the image, converts it via `cv_bridge` to an OpenCV matrix, applies Gaussian blur, converts BGR→HSV, thresholds the yellow color, and computes the centroid using `cv2.moments`. (2 marks) 3. **Action (Node → Gazebo):** The lateral error is fed to a PID controller; the output $\omega$ is wrapped in a `geometry_msgs/msg/Twist` and published to `/cmd_vel`. The `gazebo_ros_diff_drive` plugin converts this into left/right wheel velocities. (1 mark) **Key topics:** | Topic | Type | Direction | |---|---|---| | `/camera/image_raw` | `sensor_msgs/Image` | Gazebo → Node | | `/cmd_vel` | `geometry_msgs/Twist` | Node → Gazebo | | `/odom` | `nav_msgs/Odometry` | Gazebo → Node (optional) | | `/tf` | `tf2_msgs/TFMessage` | Gazebo → Node | **(b)** Derive the discrete-time PID law used in the node and justify the negative sign in $\omega(t) = -(K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e})$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Starting from the continuous PID equation $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}(t)$ (1 mark), apply backward-Euler discretization with period $T_s$: $$\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{k=0}^{n} e(k)$$ $$\dot{e}(t) \;\approx\; \frac{e(n) - e(n-1)}{T_s}$$ Substituting (1 mark): $$\omega(n) = K_p e(n) + K_i T_s \sum_{k=0}^{n} e(k) + K_d \frac{e(n) - e(n-1)}{T_s}$$ The **negative sign** is justified as follows (3 marks): the camera's $x$-axis points to the **right** of the robot. If the line appears to the **right** of center, $x_{\text{centroid}} > x_c$, making the error $e = x_c - x_{\text{centroid}}$ **negative**. To steer the robot **right** to chase the line, ROS requires a **negative** $\omega$ (right-hand rule: positive $\omega$ is counter-clockwise = left turn). Therefore $\omega = -K_p e$ ensures the steering sign matches the line's position. (2 marks) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain how `cv2.moments()` is used to extract the line centroid. What happens when $M_{00} = 0$, and how should the node handle it? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** `cv2.moments(mask)` returns a dictionary of 24 spatial moments of the binary image. (1 mark) The moments $M_{00}$, $M_{10}$, and $M_{01}$ represent the area, the first moment along $x$, and the first moment along $y$, respectively: $$M_{00} = \sum \sum M(x,y), \quad M_{10} = \sum \sum x M(x,y), \quad M_{01} = \sum \sum y M(x,y)$$ (2 marks) The centroid is then: $$(x_c, y_c) = \left(\frac{M_{10}}{M_{00}},\; \frac{M_{01}}{M_{00}}\right)$$ (1 mark) When $M_{00} = 0$, no yellow pixels were detected — the line is **out of view** (lost, off-camera, or occluded). Division by zero would yield a `ZeroDivisionError`. (1 mark) The node should handle this in an `if M['m00'] > 0:` block: command the robot to **stop forward motion** and **rotate in place** (e.g., $\omega = +0.3$ rad/s) until the line re-enters the field of view and $M_{00} > 0$ again. (2 marks) **(b)** Compare HSV thresholding with Canny edge detection for line detection. State two advantages and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **HSV Thresholding** (3 marks): - *Advantage 1:* Extremely fast — only two `inRange` operations, no per-pixel gradient computation. - *Advantage 2:* Robust to changes in **brightness** because the Hue channel decouples color from intensity. - *Disadvantage:* Requires the line color to be **known a priori** and distinct from the floor; fails under dramatic shadows. **Canny Edge Detection** (3 marks): - *Advantage 1:* **Color-independent** — works on any high-contrast line, white-on-black or black-on-white. - *Advantage 2:* Detects **multiple line segments**, useful for intersections and forks. - *Disadvantage:* Generates many false edges (tiles, textures); needs **Hough Transform** post-processing, which is computationally heavier and slower for real-time control. (1 mark for the comparative summary) --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in the KTU Valuation Key:** > 1. **Missing `cv_bridge` import or incorrect encoding** — students write `desired_encoding='rgb8'` instead of `'bgr8'`, causing color channels to swap and the line to never be detected. **Penalty: 1 mark.** > 2. **No divide-by-zero check** on `M['m00']` — the node crashes on the first frame the line is lost. Examiners treat this as a **fatal bug** (-2 marks). > 3. **Wrong sign convention** — students forget the negative sign and the robot turns **away** from the line. This is a logic error worth **-1 mark** unless justified. > 4. **Forgetting to saturate** $\omega$ — un-clipped outputs make Gazebo's diff-drive model explode with `NaN` velocities. **Penalty: 1 mark.** > 5. **Not using `Twist()`** — publishing a raw float instead of a `Twist` message silently fails because `/cmd_vel` expects `Twist`. **Penalty: 1 mark.** > 6. **Hardcoding `image_center = 320`** — this is fragile. Award the bonus mark (out-of-syllabus) only if the student reads the width from the message: `self.image_center = msg.width // 2`. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for the night before the exam):** > - **Topics involved:** `/camera/image_raw`, `/cmd_vel`, `cv_bridge`, `cv2.inRange`, `cv2.moments`, `Twist`, `rclpy.spin`. > - **Message types:** `sensor_msgs/msg/Image`, `geometry_msgs/msg/Twist`. > - **Launch command:** `ros2 launch turtlebot3_gazebo turtlebot3_world.launch.py`. > - **Default linear velocity:** $v = 0.2$ m/s; **angular max:** $1.0$ rad/s. > - **PID gains (working baseline):** $K_p = 0.005$, $K_d = 0.003$, $K_i = 0.0001$. > - **Error formula:** $e = x_{\text{image center}} - x_{\text{centroid}}$. > - **Output formula:** $\omega = -K_p e - K_i \int e\,dt - K_d \dot{e}$. > - **Always** check `M['m00'] > 0` **before** dividing. > - **Always** clip $\omega$ to $\pm 1.0$ rad/s. > - **Always** publish a `Twist` object, never a raw float. > - **Color choice for default world:** yellow line on grey floor (Hue 20-30 in OpenCV HSV). > - **Control rate:** matches the subscriber queue rate (10 Hz is sufficient for 0.2 m/s). > - **If the robot is wiggling:** reduce $K_p$ or **increase** $K_d$. > - **If the robot drifts off on straight sections:** add a small $K_i$ (start with $0.0001$). > - **If the line is lost:** rotate in place with $\omega = +0.3$ rad/s; never stop completely.

topics.title: Create a new mobile robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL507 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Create a new mobile robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** ecosystem, **creating a new mobile robot** refers to the systematic process of designing a robot's physical and kinematic description — namely its geometry, inertial properties, joint linkages, sensor placements, and transmission mechanisms — using the **Unified Robot Description Format (URDF)**. A URDF is an **XML-based file format** that mathematically models a robot as a **kinematic tree of links (rigid bodies)** connected by **joints (kinematic constraints)**, enabling the ROS toolchain (TF, robot_state_publisher, RViz, Gazebo) to understand, visualize, and simulate the robot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A mobile robot in ROS is *not* a static model. It must be defined with **(1) URDF/Xacro** for structure, **(2) Gazebo plugins** for physics/sensors, **(3) ros2_control** or `diff_drive_controller` for actuation, and **(4) launch files** for orchestration. Mastering all four pillars is mandatory for the lab record and viva.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **remote-controlled toy car** from scratch.

- **The Chassis (Base Link)** → The car's body frame. Without this, nothing else has a place to live.
- **The Wheels (Child Links)** → Four separate parts that need to be attached to the chassis via axles.
- **The Axles (Joints)** → The mechanical pivots. They are *not* the wheels themselves; they are the *constraints* telling ROS how the wheels are allowed to move relative to the chassis.
- **The Motor Specs (Inertial + Transmission)** → The physical properties (mass, moment of inertia) and the gear ratios that translate electrical signals into wheel rotation.
- **The Camera/Sensor (Gazebo Plugins)** → The "eyes" of the car that feed data into ROS topics.

A **URDF file** is essentially a **bill of materials + assembly manual** for this car, written in XML so that ROS can "read" it and bring the robot to life in simulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** URDF describes the robot **kinematically** (how parts are arranged and how they move), not how it is *controlled*. Control is layered on top using `ros2_control` and controller plugins.

## Standard Metrics and Naming Conventions (KTU Convention)

- **Base link naming:** Conventionally `base_link` — the canonical root of the TF tree.
- **Coordinate frame:** **REP-103** standard: **X-forward, Y-left, Z-up** for mobile robots.
- **Standard units:** Meters (length), kilograms (mass), radians (angles), SI throughout.
- **Wheel separation:** Typical 2WD differential drive uses ~**0.287 m** (e.g., TurtleBot3 Burger).
- **Wheel radius:** Typical ~**0.033 m** for small educational robots.

> [!TIP]
> Always validate your URDF with `check_urdf <robot_name>.urdf` before launching. A single malformed joint tag will silently break the TF tree in RViz.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Four Pillars of a Mobile Robot in ROS 2

### Pillar 1: URDF (Unified Robot Description Format)

A URDF file is composed of two primary XML elements:
1. **`<link>` tags** — define rigid bodies (visual, collision, inertial).
2. **`<joint>` tags** — define how two links are connected (type, axis, limits).

Each `<link>` contains three sub-tags:
- **`<visual>`** — How the robot *looks* in RViz (geometry + material).
- **`<collision>`** — How the robot *interacts* with the environment in Gazebo.
- **`<inertial>`** — Mass distribution, critical for physics simulation.

Each `<joint>` requires:
- **`name`** — unique identifier.
- **`type`** — `revolute`, `continuous`, `prismatic`, `fixed`, `floating`, or `planar`.
- **`<parent>`** and **`<child>`** — defines the kinematic hierarchy.
- **`<origin>`** — the static transform (xyz + rpy) from parent to child.
- **`<axis>`** — the rotation/translation axis (in parent frame).
- **`<limit>`** — for `revolute`/`prismatic` joints: lower, upper, effort, velocity.

### Pillar 2: Differential Drive Kinematics (The "Why" Behind the Motion)

A 2-wheel differential drive robot moves by commanding different velocities to its left and right wheels. The relationship between wheel velocities and robot body velocity is governed by:

$$v = r \cdot \frac{(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$

$$\omega = \frac{r}{L} \cdot (\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

Where:
- $v$ = linear velocity of the robot center (m/s)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of the robot (rad/s)
- $r$ = wheel radius (m)
- $L$ = wheel separation / track width (m)
- $\omega_R, \omega_L$ = right and left wheel angular velocities (rad/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **inverse kinematics** mapping. The `diff_drive_controller` package internally uses the **forward kinematics** to convert commanded body velocities back into individual wheel commands.

### Pillar 3: Gazebo Plugins (Bridging URDF to Physics)

A URDF alone is a static description. To make it move in Gazebo, you embed **Gazebo-specific tags** inside the URDF:

- **`<gazebo>` reference tags** — attach plugins to specific links/joints.
- **`diff_drive_plugin`** — subscribes to `cmd_vel` and drives the wheels.
- **`laser`/`camera`/`imu` plugins** — publish sensor data on ROS topics.

### Pillar 4: Launch Files (Python/XML orchestration)

Launch files are the **entry point** of any ROS 2 robot. They start:
- `robot_state_publisher` — broadcasts TFs from URDF.
- Gazebo simulator.
- The robot spawn entity.
- All controllers and bridges.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Drive Linear Velocity | $v = r \cdot (\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$ | $v$ [m/s], $r$ [m], $\omega$ [rad/s] | Mobile robot odometry & control |
| Differential Drive Angular Velocity | $\omega = (r/L) \cdot (\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | $L$ [m] = wheel separation | Turning rate calculation |
| Forward Kinematics (body to wheel) | $\omega_R = (v + \omega L/2)/r$ | — | Controller output |
| Forward Kinematics (body to wheel) | $\omega_L = (v - \omega L/2)/r$ | — | Controller output |
| URDF Joint Types | `revolute`, `continuous`, `prismatic`, `fixed`, `floating`, `planar` | — | Selecting correct kinematic constraint |
| REP-103 Frame (Mobile) | X-forward, Y-left, Z-up | — | Standard ROS convention |
| Inertia Tensor (Solid Cylinder) | $I_{xx} = I_{yy} = (mr^2)/4 + (mh^2)/12$ | $m$ [kg], $r,h$ [m] | Required in `<inertial>` tag |
| Inertia Tensor (Solid Cylinder) | $I_{zz} = (mr^2)/2$ | — | Rotational inertia about wheel axis |
| TF Tree Root | Always `base_link` | — | Kinematic tree anchor |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivative 1: Deriving the Differential Drive Kinematic Model

We start from the **instantaneous center of rotation (ICR)** principle. For a 2WD robot with wheel separation $L$ and wheel radius $r$:

### Step 1 — Define the wheel contact velocities

The linear velocity at the right wheel contact point on the ground is:
$$v_R = r \cdot \omega_R$$

Similarly, the left wheel linear velocity is:
$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L$$

### Step 2 — Express robot body velocity at its center

Since the robot's center lies exactly midway between the two wheels, by linearity of the velocity field along the axle:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and simplify

Substituting Step 1 into Step 2:

$$v = \frac{r \cdot \omega_R + r \cdot \omega_L}{2}$$

$$\boxed{v = r \cdot \frac{(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}}$$

### Step 4 — Derive the angular velocity

The robot rotates about an ICR located at distance $R_{ICR}$ from the center, perpendicular to the axle. The two wheels have velocities $v_R$ and $v_L$ in opposite senses relative to the rotation. Therefore:

$$v_R - v_L = L \cdot \omega$$

Solving for $\omega$:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r \cdot \omega_R - r \cdot \omega_L}{L}$$

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{r}{L} \cdot (\omega_R - \omega_L)}$$

### Step 5 — Invert the model (forward kinematics for control)

To command a desired $(v_{cmd}, \omega_{cmd})$ from `cmd_vel`, the controller must compute individual wheel angular velocities:

$$\omega_R = \frac{v_{cmd} + \omega_{cmd} \cdot L/2}{r}$$

$$\omega_L = \frac{v_{cmd} - \omega_{cmd} \cdot L/2}{r}$$

This is the equation implemented inside the `diff_drive_controller` plugin.

## Derivative 2: Inertia Tensor for a Solid Cylindrical Wheel

A solid cylinder of mass $m$, radius $r$, and height $h$ (axle along $z$) has the principal moments of inertia:

$$I_{xx} = I_{yy} = \frac{1}{4}mr^2 + \frac{1}{12}mh^2$$

$$I_{zz} = \frac{1}{2}mr^2$$

**Derivation outline** (for KTU viva):

- $I_{zz}$ comes from a uniform disk: $\int_0^r \int_0^{2\pi} (\rho h)(s^2) s \, d\theta \, ds = (1/2)mr^2$.
- $I_{xx} = I_{yy}$ uses the perpendicular axis theorem: $I_{xx} = I_{yy} = (1/2)(I_{zz} + m(h/2)^2)$ for a cylinder centered at origin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Record Mandate:** When documenting this experiment, you MUST show this derivation in your record. Examiners frequently award full marks only when both the kinematic *and* inertia derivations are present.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define URDF. List any four joint types supported by URDF.**

**Model Answer:**
URDF (Unified Robot Description Format) is an XML specification used in ROS to describe a robot's physical structure — its links, joints, sensors, and visual/collision properties — as a kinematic tree. Four joint types:
1. `fixed` — no movement
2. `revolute` — rotation with limits
3. `continuous` — unlimited rotation
4. `prismatic` — linear sliding

*(Acceptable: `floating`, `planar`)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the role of `robot_state_publisher` node in a mobile robot setup?**

**Model Answer:**
The `robot_state_publisher` node reads the URDF file, subscribes to `/joint_states`, computes the forward kinematics for all joints, and broadcasts the resulting **TF transforms** between every link on the `/tf` topic. This allows RViz, Gazebo, and navigation stacks to understand the robot's current configuration in 3D space.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure of a URDF file. With a neat block diagram, describe the kinematic tree of a 2-wheeled differential drive mobile robot with at least one caster and a LiDAR sensor.

**Model Solution:**

A URDF file begins with a `<robot name="...">` root element containing:
- Multiple `<link>` elements (each defining visual, collision, inertial).
- Multiple `<joint>` elements (each connecting a parent link to a child link).

For the **2WD mobile robot**, the kinematic tree is:

```
base_footprint (fixed to odom, conceptual root for navigation)
   └── base_link (the chassis)
         ├── wheel_left_link (continuous joint, axis = +X)
         ├── wheel_right_link (continuous joint, axis = +X)
         ├── caster_wheel_link (fixed joint, passive)
         └── laser_mount_link (fixed joint)
                └── laser_link (fixed joint, houses LiDAR)
```

Each wheel joint is of type `continuous` with the rotation axis along the X direction (REP-103). The caster is a `fixed` joint in URDF but is **passive in Gazebo** via friction parameters. The LiDAR is rigidly attached to a mount on top of the base_link.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the forward and inverse kinematic equations of a differential drive mobile robot. Given wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, wheel separation $L = 0.30$ m, and a commanded body velocity of $v = 0.20$ m/s, $\omega = 1.0$ rad/s, compute the required individual wheel angular velocities.

**Model Solution:**

**Inverse Kinematics (wheel velocities to body velocity):**

$$v = r \cdot \frac{(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$\omega = \frac{r}{L} \cdot (\omega_R - \omega_L) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Forward Kinematics (body velocity to wheel velocities):**

From (1) and (2), solving the system for $\omega_R$ and $\omega_L$:

Multiply (2) by $L/2$:

$$\omega \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R - \omega_L) \quad \text{...(3)}$$

Add (1) divided by 1 (multiplied by $r/2$):

$$\frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) = v \quad \text{...(4)}$$

Adding (3) and (4):

$$v + \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2} = r \cdot \omega_R$$

$$\therefore \omega_R = \frac{v + \omega L/2}{r}$$

Subtracting (3) from (4):

$$v - \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2} = r \cdot \omega_L$$

$$\therefore \omega_L = \frac{v - \omega L/2}{r}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $v = 0.20$ m/s, $\omega = 1.0$ rad/s.

$$\omega_R = \frac{0.20 + (1.0)(0.30/2)}{0.05} = \frac{0.20 + 0.15}{0.05} = \frac{0.35}{0.05} = 7.0 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_L = \frac{0.20 - (1.0)(0.30/2)}{0.05} = \frac{0.20 - 0.15}{0.05} = \frac{0.05}{0.05} = 1.0 \text{ rad/s}$$

Since $\omega_R > \omega_L$, the robot **turns left** (counter-clockwise), consistent with positive $\omega$.

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the four essential components required to bring up a new mobile robot in ROS 2? Explain the role of each with reference to a differential drive example.

**Model Solution:**

1. **URDF/Xacro file** — Defines the robot's physical structure (links, joints, sensors, inertia). Example: defines the chassis, two drive wheels, a caster, and a LiDAR link.
2. **Gazebo plugins inside URDF** — Provide physics behavior. The `diff_drive_plugin` reads `/cmd_vel` and applies forces to the wheel joints. The `laser_plugin` simulates the LiDAR and publishes `/scan`.
3. **ros2_control configuration** — `controllers.yaml` file declaring the `diff_drive_controller` and `joint_state_broadcaster`. This decouples hardware abstraction from the control algorithm.
4. **Launch file** — A Python file using `launch_ros` APIs to start Gazebo, spawn the robot, load controllers, and start `robot_state_publisher` in a single command.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 4-wheeled skid-steer mobile robot has wheel radius $r = 0.10$ m, wheelbase $L = 0.40$ m, and track $W = 0.35$ m. Compute the wheel angular velocities for a desired $v = 0.50$ m/s and $\omega = 0.50$ rad/s (turning right).

**Model Solution:**

For a 4-wheel skid-steer, the kinematic model approximates as differential drive using the **effective track** $W$:

$$v_{right} = v - \omega \cdot \frac{W}{2}$$

$$v_{left} = v + \omega \cdot \frac{W}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$v_{right} = 0.50 - (0.50)(0.35/2) = 0.50 - 0.0875 = 0.4125 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_{left} = 0.50 + 0.0875 = 0.5875 \text{ m/s}$$

The corresponding wheel angular velocities:

$$\omega_{right} = \frac{v_{right}}{r} = \frac{0.4125}{0.10} = 4.125 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_{left} = \frac{v_{left}}{r} = \frac{0.5875}{0.10} = 5.875 \text{ rad/s}$$

Since $\omega_{left} > \omega_{right}$, the robot turns **right** (clockwise), as expected.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing parent/child in joint tags** — The wheel is the *child*, the chassis (`base_link`) is the *parent*. Reversing this breaks the TF tree.
> 2. **Missing `<inertial>` block** — Gazebo will refuse to spawn the robot or apply zero mass, causing it to fall through the ground.
> 3. **Wrong joint axis** — A common error is setting the wheel rotation axis to Z instead of X. REP-103 mandates X-forward, so wheel axis MUST be X.
> 4. **Forgetting `continuous` type for wheels** — Using `revolute` without limits causes the `joint_state_publisher` to clamp wheel motion. Use `continuous` for unconstrained spinning wheels.
> 5. **Unit mismatch in `cmd_vel`** — Linear is in **m/s**, angular in **rad/s**, not degrees.
> 6. **Not running `check_urdf`** before launch — Always validate. URDF errors are silent and only surface in RViz as missing transforms.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **URDF** is the **XML kinematic description** of a robot — links + joints + sensors.
- A **link** = rigid body with `<visual>`, `<collision>`, and `<inertial>`.
- A **joint** = kinematic constraint between parent and child link.
- **REP-103 coordinate convention:** X-forward, Y-left, Z-up (mobile robots).
- **Wheel joints** must be of type `continuous` with axis = `<axis xyz="1 0 0"/>`.
- **Caster wheels** are modeled as `fixed` joints in URDF but with low friction in Gazebo.
- **Differential Drive Kinematics:**
  - $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$
  - $\omega = (r/L)(\omega_R - \omega_L)$
- **Inverse mapping** is what the `diff_drive_controller` computes to drive the wheels.
- **Four pillars** to make a robot "alive" in ROS 2: **URDF + Gazebo plugins + ros2_control + Launch file**.
- **`robot_state_publisher`** broadcasts TFs from URDF + `/joint_states`.
- **Always validate** with `check_urdf` and visualize in RViz before Gazebo.
- **Standard units:** meters, kilograms, seconds, radians — strictly SI.
- **Sensors** (LiDAR, camera, IMU) are attached as `fixed` child links to a sensor mount.
- The `cmd_vel` topic uses `geometry_msgs/Twist`: `linear.x` (m/s) and `angular.z` (rad/s).
- **Inertia tensors** for wheels use the solid cylinder formulas; missing them = Gazebo simulation failure.
- **Launch files** in ROS 2 are Python (`.launch.py`) using `launch_ros.actions.Node` and `ExecuteProcess`.
- **TF tree must be a tree, not a graph** — every link (except root) has exactly one parent.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Create a New Mobile Robot**
> For ROS 2 lab viva, be prepared to draw the URDF kinematic tree from memory and explain the differential drive forward/inverse kinematics on the board.
